lenses.guidance: Tamar's pledge — seal, cord, staff (v 18) — and Judah's later 'please identify these' (v 25) parallel Joseph's robe in 37:32-33. The 'recognise this' structure binds the two chapters as twin scenes of unmasking.

Tamar's risk preserves the covenant line. Perez (v 29) becomes David's ancestor through Ruth 4:18-22 — the chapter is a redemptive rescue of the messianic seed when Judah was willing to let it die with Shelah.

Matthew 1:3 names Tamar in Jesus' genealogy — Perez (v 29) descends to David and the Messiah through scandal and Canaanite-widow courage. Christ's family tree refuses respectability from its first generations.

'She is more righteous than I' (v 26) — Judah's public confession is the moral hinge of his life. Repent quickly when caught; name the sin without softening; let the corrected verdict stand. Today, own what is owed.

Tamar's vindication (v 26) is echoed in Ruth's (Ruth 4:11-12 explicitly invokes her name) — outsider women preserve the covenant line. The thread runs to Rahab and Bathsheba in Matthew 1, throughout Scripture.
